answers.value: Community resource fair with kids’ activities and local vendor booths
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Describe The Type Of Entertainment That Will Be Provided
answers.value:
We are planning a full afternoon and evening of family-friendly entertainment designed to appeal to a wide range of ages and interests in the neighborhood:

1. Live Music:
- From 2:00 PM to 4:00 PM, a local acoustic duo called “Maple Street Strings” (guitar and vocals) will perform a mix of classic rock, folk, and light pop covers at moderate volume. They typically play songs from artists like The Beatles, Fleetwood Mac, and James Taylor, along with a few original songs that are clean and family-friendly.
- From 5:00 PM to 7:30 PM, a five-piece neighborhood band, “Westwood Groove Collective,” will perform upbeat soul, funk, and R&B covers. They use standard band equipment (drum kit, bass, electric guitar, keyboard, and two vocal mics) with a small PA system. Volume will be monitored to stay within reasonable residential neighborhood levels. They will also make announcements about lost children, parked cars that need to be moved, and schedule updates between sets.

2. DJ and Background Music:
- Between live band sets (approximately 4:00–5:00 PM and 7:30–8:30 PM), our volunteer DJ (a neighbor, Eric Martinez) will play a curated playlist of clean, radio-edit songs spanning oldies, Motown, 80s/90s hits, and current pop. All tracks will be screened for explicit language; we are mindful that many children will be present.
- The DJ will also facilitate short interactive activities such as a kids’ dance-off, a hula-hoop contest, and a short line-dancing segment, encouraging broad participation without any risky or inappropriate behavior.

3. Kids’ Activities and Games:
- We will have a dedicated children’s area roughly mid-block, supervised by 4–5 adult volunteers wearing matching “Event Volunteer” T-shirts. Planned activities include sidewalk chalk art, jump rope, hopscotch, beanbag toss, and a ring-toss game with small prizes (stickers, pencils, and small toys).
- From 3:00 PM to 4:00 PM, one of our neighbors, who is a part-time children’s librarian, will lead an interactive story time on picnic blankets, reading short picture books and leading simple songs and fingerplays.
- From 4:30 PM to 5:00 PM, we plan a “neighborhood talent showcase” for children and teens, where kids can perform short acts (music, magic tricks, simple dances). We will require parents/guardians to be present and will limit each act to 3–4 minutes to keep things moving and prevent overcrowding at the staging area.

4. Community Demonstrations and Resource Booths:
- The local fire department has been invited to bring a fire truck for a static display for about one hour in the late afternoon (exact time to be coordinated). Children will be able to look at the truck and ask questions; they will not be allowed to climb unattended, and firefighters will be present at all times.
- A neighborhood fitness instructor will lead two short, low-impact group sessions (one family-friendly stretching class and one light cardio/dance fitness demo) in the street, using a portable speaker at low volume.
- We will have 4–6 informational tables from local organizations (neighborhood association, after-school programs, local small businesses). These booths will primarily provide information, giveaways (pens, magnets, brochures), and sign-up sheets for mailing lists; there will be no high-pressure sales tactics.

5. Non-Amusement Activities:
- We will not have any mechanical rides, inflatables, dunk tanks, or carnival-style high-risk attractions. The focus is on simple, low-risk entertainment like music, yard games (cornhole, ladder toss), and socializing.
- Several neighbors will set up board games and card tables (e.g., checkers, chess, Uno, and dominoes) in front of their homes to encourage quieter, more relaxed interaction away from the main music area.

We will post a printed schedule of all entertainment by the food area and at both ends of the block so attendees know what to expect. All entertainment is intended to be family-friendly, non-commercial in tone, and respectful of nearby residents who may not be attending.
